title: Scrambled Eggs
description: Creamy, soft scrambled eggs — the single most useful thing a beginner can learn to cook

blocks.name: Scrambled Eggs
blocks.description: The most important thing to learn here is not the recipe — it is the technique. Low heat and patience turn two eggs into something genuinely delicious.

items.title: Beat the eggs
items.body: Crack the eggs into a bowl. Add a pinch of salt and pepper, then beat with a fork until the yolks and whites are fully combined with no streaks remaining.

items.title: Melt the butter slowly
items.body: Place a non-stick frying pan over **medium-low heat**. Add the butter and let it melt gently, tilting the pan to coat the base. Do not let the butter bubble aggressively or brown.

items.title: Cook slowly, keep moving
items.body: Pour in the eggs. Leave them for about 20–30 seconds until you see the edges just beginning to set, then use a spatula to gently push the eggs from the edges toward the centre. Continue doing this every 15–20 seconds — you are not scrambling frantically, just folding slowly.

items.title: Pull off the heat early
items.body: When the eggs look **mostly set but still slightly glossy and wet** (about 3–4 minutes total), take the pan off the heat. The eggs will finish cooking from the residual heat of the pan in the next 30 seconds. Stir in any cheese or toppings now.

items.title: Serve immediately
items.body: Transfer straight onto a warm plate. Scrambled eggs do not wait — serve them at once with toast, or alongside grilled tomatoes and mushrooms.

blocks.title: The most common mistake
blocks.content: High heat produces rubbery, watery eggs. If your eggs are cooking faster than you can keep up with, take the pan off the heat mid-cook to slow things down — you can always put it back.

blocks.title: Once you have the basics down
blocks.content:
Try folding in one of the following just before you take the pan off the heat:

- **Cheese** — cheddar, feta, or soft goat cheese
- **Fresh herbs** — chives, parsley, or dill
- **Vegetables** — a small handful of wilted spinach or diced tomato
- **Meat** — crumbled cooked bacon or diced ham
